title: Flex Day 6
text:
Experiment 1: Roll your die 7 times. Count the number of 2’s.

Let \(X\) be the random variable that counts the number of 2’s in 7 rolls of a fair six-sided die.

Compute \(P(X = 12)\).

What are the values that will have nonzero probability?



Note: \(X\) is a Bionomial Random Variable
Let’s plot the probability model for a Binomial Random Variable
f1 <- function(x,n=20,p=0.5){
  # x must be an whole number between 0 and n, endpoints included
  factorial(n)/(factorial(x)*factorial(n-x))*p^x*(1-p)^(n-x)
}

n <- 20
p <- 0.25
x <- 0:n
barplot(f1(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)
We can simulate this random variable using the rbinom() function in R.
tmp <- rbinom(10,7,1/6)

tmp <- rbinom(100000,7,1/6)
Let’s take a look this data
hist(tmp,probability=TRUE)
- Use your simulated data to compute $P(X=4)$.
1. count the number of 4's in the simulated data
obs <- length(which(tmp == 4))
1. count how many simulated data
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot
- Use your simulated data to compute $P(X > 5.3)$.
1. count the number of 6's and 7's in the simulated data
obs <- length(which(tmp > 5.3))
1. count how many simulated data
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot

1. Calculating this another way... P(X > 5.3) = 1 - P(X <= 5.3)
1. count the number of 0's, 1's, 2's, 3's, 4's, and 5's in the simulated data
obs <- length(which(tmp < 5.3))
1. count how many simulated data
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot
1-prob
Let’s calculate the mean, variance, and standard deviation of our data.
mean(tmp)

var(tmp)

sd(tmp)

sqrt(var(tmp))
Experiement 2: Roll your die until you roll a 4. Count the total number of rolls required to get the 4, including the last roll in which the 4 appeared.

Let \(X\) be the random variable that counts the number of rolls until you roll a 4 using a fair six-sided die.

Compute \(P(X = -3)\).

What are the values that will have nonzero probability?



Note: \(X\) is a Geometric Random Variable
Let’s plot the probability model for a Geometric Random Variable
f2 <- function(x,p=0.5){
  # x must be an positive integer
  p*(1-p)^(x-1)
}

p <- 0.25
x <- 1:50
barplot(f2(x,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)
We can simulate this random variable using the rgeom() function in R.
tmp <- rgeom(10,1/6)+1

tmp <- rgeom(100000,1/6)+1

1. Why +1? This has to do with matching the why the probability model is programed in R with the way the probability model is given here.
Let’s take a look this data
hist(tmp,probability=TRUE)
- Use your simulated data to compute $P(X=3)$.
1. count the number of 4's in the simulated data
obs <- length(which(tmp == 3))
1. count how many simulated data
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot
- Use your simulated data to compute the probability you have to roll the die at least 10 times before you get a 4.
1. P(X > 10) = 1 - P(X <= 10)
1. count the number of 1's, 2's, 3's, 4's, 5's, 6's, 7's, 8's, 9's, and 10's in the simulated data
obs <- length(which(tmp <= 10))
1. count how many simulated data
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot
1-prob
Let’s calculate the mean, variance, and standard deviation of our data.
mean(tmp)

var(tmp)

sd(tmp)

sqrt(var(tmp))




\(f_1(x; n,p) = \frac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}p^x(1-p)^{ (n-x) }\) with \(x = 0, 1, 2, 3, ... , n\) where \(n\) is a positive integer and \(0 \leq p \leq 1\).

This function is a called Binomial Distribution. It contains probability information about a Binomial random variable.
In R we use the rbinom() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_2(x; p) = p(1-p)^{ (x-1) }\) with $x = 1, 2, 3, … $ where \(0 \leq p \leq 1\).

This function is called a Geometric Distribution. It contains probability information about a Geometric random variable.
In R we use the rgeom() function +1 to simulate this random variable.

\(f_3(x; \lambda) = e^{-\lambda}\frac{\lambda^x}{x!}\) with $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, … $ where \(\lambda > 0\).

This function is called a Poisson Distribution. It contains probability information about a Poisson random variable.
In R we use the rpois() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_4(x; \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(x > 0\) where \(\lambda >0\).

This function is called an Exponential Distribution. It contains probability information about an Exponential random variable.
In R we use the rexp() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_5(x; \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}},\) where \(\mu\) is a real number and \(\sigma > 0\).

This function is called a Normal Distribution. It contains probability information about a Normal random variable.
In R we use the rnorm() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_6(x; a,b) = \frac{1}{b-a}\), with \(a \leq x \leq b\) where \(a\) and \(b\) are real numbers.

This function is called a Uniform Distribution. It contains probability information about a Uniform random variable.
In R we use the runif() function to simulate this random variable.


You can use the following code to define these functions in R.
f1 <- function(x,n=20,p=0.5){
1. x must be an whole number between 0 and n, endpoints included
  factorial(n)/(factorial(x)*factorial(n-x))*p^x*(1-p)^(n-x)
}

f2 <- function(x,p=0.5){
1. x must be an postive integer
  p*(1-p)^(x-1)
}

f3 <- function(x,lambda=1){
1. x must be a nonegative integer
  exp(-lambda)*lambda^x/factorial(x)
}

f4 <- function(x,lambda=1){
1. x must be positive
  lambda*exp(-lambda*x)
}

f5 <- function(x,mu=0,s=1){
  (1/sqrt(2*pi*s^2))*exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2*s^2))
}

f6 <- function(x,a=0,b=1){
  
  out <- rep(0,length(x))
  out[(a <= x) & (x <= b)] <- 0*x[(a <= x) & (x <= b)] + 1/(b-a)

  return(out)
}
You can use the following code to plot these functions in R.
1. f1 has discrete inputs 
n <- 30
p <- 0.4
x <- 0:n
barplot(f1(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f2 has discrete inputs
p <- 0.2
x <- 1:50
barplot(f2(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f3 has discrete inputs
lambda <- 12
x <- 0:50
barplot(f3(x,lambda),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f4 has continuous inputs
lambda <- 3
x <- seq(0,5,0.01)
plot(x,f4(x,lambda),type='l')

1. f5 has continuous inputs
mu <- 3
s <- 0.2
x <- seq(-1,7,0.001)
plot(x,f5(x,mu,s),type='l')

1. f6 has continuous inputs
a <- -4
b <- 2
x <- seq(-5,5,0.01)
plot(x,f6(x,a,b),type='l')




Give each group member 1-2 minutes to share what they prepared from the between class work.
Work on the chalkboard with your group. Everyone needs to practice communicating mathematics (sharing their ideas, writing mathematics, articulating what they understand, articulating what they don’t understand yet). Everyone needs practice listening and contributing to their group by engaging with their peers’ work. Remember to Pass The Chalk between each problem, giving everyone a chance to act as scribe. When you are the scribe, it’s fine to not know exactly how to proceed. The goal is to ask questions of each other and help each other.
Leave up your work so that you can compare your answers with your neighbors. If you notice an answer differs from a neighboring group’s, then have a discussion with them.
Take turns writing your solutions on the chalk board and discussing any questions. Show your work, step-by-step, listing the rules you use at each stage.
Let’s play with simulation in R some more. 1. Select one of the following common probability models. - Poisson - Exponential - Normal - Uniform

Adapt the following code to plot the probability model you selected.

1. If the inputs are discrete use and adapt this code.
f1 <- function(x,n=20,p=0.5){
  # x must be an whole number between 0 and n, endpoints included
  factorial(n)/(factorial(x)*factorial(n-x))*p^x*(1-p)^(n-x)
}

n <- 20
p <- 0.25
x <- 0:n
barplot(f1(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)
1. If the inputs are continuous use and adapt this code.
f4 <- function(x,lambda=1){
  # x must be positive
  lambda*exp(-lambda*x)
}

lambda <- 3
x <- seq(0,5,0.01)
plot(x,f4(x,lambda),type='l')

Simulate this random variable you selected by adapting the code below.

tmp <- rbinom(100000,7,1/6)

Visualize your simulated data by creating a histogram.
Calculate the mean, variance, and standard deviation for your simulated data.
Adapt the following code to approximate the following probabilities using your simulated data.

\(P(X < 3)\)
\(P(X > 6)\)


obs <- length(which(tmp < 10))
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot

Select another model and repeat.





Deterministic models describe relationships between quantities.

time a light bulb is on (quantity 1) and the brightness of the bulb (quantity 2)
number of months since purchase (quantity 1) and the thickness of a shoe tread (quantity 2)
distance traveled (quantity 2) and wear on vehicle (quantity 2)
amount of butter (quantity 1) and chewiness of cookie (quantity 2)

Probability models describe probability information for a measurement.

Binomial distribution

number of “successes” in \(n\) trials

Geometric distribution

number of trials until the first success

Poisson distribution

number of events during a fixed time interval (assuming events occur at a constant rate)

Exponential distribution

amount of time until the next event happens (assuming events occur at a constant rate)

Normal distribution

a machine part is manufactured to a specified length with some tolerance (most parts will be close to the specified length with some parts longer or shorter)

Uniform distribution

every value has an equal chance of getting picked

This function is a called Binomial Distribution. It contains probability information about a Binomial random variable.
In R we use the rbinom() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_2(x; p) = p(1-p)^{ (x-1) }\) with $x = 1, 2, 3, … $ where \(0 \leq p \leq 1\).

This function is called a Geometric Distribution. It contains probability information about a Geometric random variable.
In R we use the rgeom() function +1 to simulate this random variable.

\(f_3(x; \lambda) = e^{-\lambda}\frac{\lambda^x}{x!}\) with $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, … $ where \(\lambda > 0\).

This function is called a Poisson Distribution. It contains probability information about a Poisson random variable.
In R we use the rpois() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_4(x; \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(x > 0\) where \(\lambda >0\).

This function is called an Exponential Distribution. It contains probability information about an Exponential random variable.
In R we use the rexp() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_5(x; \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}},\) where \(\mu\) is a real number and \(\sigma > 0\).

This function is called a Normal Distribution. It contains probability information about a Normal random variable.
In R we use the rnorm() function to simulate this random variable.

\(f_6(x; a,b) = \frac{1}{b-a}\), with \(a \leq x \leq b\) where \(a\) and \(b\) are real numbers.

This function is called a Uniform Distribution. It contains probability information about a Uniform random variable.
In R we use the runif() function to simulate this random variable.


You can use the following code to define these functions in R.
f1 <- function(x,n=20,p=0.5){
1. x must be an whole number between 0 and n, endpoints included
  factorial(n)/(factorial(x)*factorial(n-x))*p^x*(1-p)^(n-x)
}

f2 <- function(x,p=0.5){
1. x must be an postive integer
  p*(1-p)^(x-1)
}

f3 <- function(x,lambda=1){
1. x must be a nonegative integer
  exp(-lambda)*lambda^x/factorial(x)
}

f4 <- function(x,lambda=1){
1. x must be positive
  lambda*exp(-lambda*x)
}

f5 <- function(x,mu=0,s=1){
  (1/sqrt(2*pi*s^2))*exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2*s^2))
}

f6 <- function(x,a=0,b=1){
  
  out <- rep(0,length(x))
  out[(a <= x) & (x <= b)] <- 0*x[(a <= x) & (x <= b)] + 1/(b-a)

  return(out)
}
You can use the following code to plot these functions in R.
1. f1 has discrete inputs 
n <- 30
p <- 0.4
x <- 0:n
barplot(f1(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f2 has discrete inputs
p <- 0.2
x <- 1:50
barplot(f2(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f3 has discrete inputs
lambda <- 12
x <- 0:50
barplot(f3(x,lambda),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)

1. f4 has continuous inputs
lambda <- 3
x <- seq(0,5,0.01)
plot(x,f4(x,lambda),type='l')

1. f5 has continuous inputs
mu <- 3
s <- 0.2
x <- seq(-1,7,0.001)
plot(x,f5(x,mu,s),type='l')

1. f6 has continuous inputs
a <- -4
b <- 2
x <- seq(-5,5,0.01)
plot(x,f6(x,a,b),type='l')

title: Flex Day 6
text:
Give each group member 1-2 minutes to share what they prepared from the between class work.
Work on the chalkboard with your group. Everyone needs to practice communicating mathematics (sharing their ideas, writing mathematics, articulating what they understand, articulating what they don’t understand yet). Everyone needs practice listening and contributing to their group by engaging with their peers’ work. Remember to Pass The Chalk between each problem, giving everyone a chance to act as scribe. When you are the scribe, it’s fine to not know exactly how to proceed. The goal is to ask questions of each other and help each other.
Leave up your work so that you can compare your answers with your neighbors. If you notice an answer differs from a neighboring group’s, then have a discussion with them.
Take turns writing your solutions on the chalk board and discussing any questions. Show your work, step-by-step, listing the rules you use at each stage.
Let’s play with simulation in R some more. 1. Select one of the following common probability models. - Poisson - Exponential - Normal - Uniform

Adapt the following code to plot the probability model you selected.

1. If the inputs are discrete use and adapt this code.
f1 <- function(x,n=20,p=0.5){
  # x must be an whole number between 0 and n, endpoints included
  factorial(n)/(factorial(x)*factorial(n-x))*p^x*(1-p)^(n-x)
}

n <- 20
p <- 0.25
x <- 0:n
barplot(f1(x,n,p),ylim=c(0,1),width=rep(1,length(x)),space=1)
1. If the inputs are continuous use and adapt this code.
f4 <- function(x,lambda=1){
  # x must be positive
  lambda*exp(-lambda*x)
}

lambda <- 3
x <- seq(0,5,0.01)
plot(x,f4(x,lambda),type='l')

Simulate this random variable you selected by adapting the code below.

tmp <- rbinom(100000,7,1/6)

Visualize your simulated data by creating a histogram.
Calculate the mean, variance, and standard deviation for your simulated data.
Adapt the following code to approximate the following probabilities using your simulated data.

\(P(X < 3)\)
\(P(X > 6)\)


obs <- length(which(tmp < 10))
tot <- length(tmp)

prob <- obs/tot

Select another model and repeat.

title: Flex Day 6
text:
Deterministic models describe relationships between quantities.

time a light bulb is on (quantity 1) and the brightness of the bulb (quantity 2)
number of months since purchase (quantity 1) and the thickness of a shoe tread (quantity 2)
distance traveled (quantity 2) and wear on vehicle (quantity 2)
amount of butter (quantity 1) and chewiness of cookie (quantity 2)

Probability models describe probability information for a measurement.

Binomial distribution

number of “successes” in \(n\) trials

Geometric distribution

number of trials until the first success

Poisson distribution

number of events during a fixed time interval (assuming events occur at a constant rate)

Exponential distribution

amount of time until the next event happens (assuming events occur at a constant rate)

Normal distribution

a machine part is manufactured to a specified length with some tolerance (most parts will be close to the specified length with some parts longer or shorter)

Uniform distribution

every value has an equal chance of getting picked

title: Flex Day 5
text:
Deterministic Models

When we study a relationship between quantities, we gather observations of the relationship. These observations are a list of ordered pairs with elements \((x_i, y_i)\). We want to study a “true” relationship, one we don’t know but we believe exists and we are trying to understand. One tool we can use to study the relationship is mathematical modeling. We can consider one (or more) mathematical model(s) and fit the model(s) to the data. When we fit a model to data we assume the relationship we are considering is (or can be) described by the model. Once we fit a model(s) to data we can use the “specific” model (or fitted model) to answer questions about the relationship (or use the assumed relationship to predict or extrapolate).



Stochastic Models

Similarly, when we study a measurement with uncertainty, we gather observations of the measurement. These observations are a list of measurements with elements \(x_i\). We want to study a “true” random variable, one we don’t know but we want to understand. Recall that a random variable is a collection of possible values with associated probability information. One tool we can use to study the unknown random variable is mathematical modeling. We can consider one (or more) distribution(s). We compare the observations of the unknown random variable to common random variables we know, thus using these distributions as models. When we fit a distribution (or model) to the observed values (data) we assume the unknown random variable is (or could be) the specific type of random variable described by the distribution. Once we fit a distribution(s) to data we can use the “specific” distribution (or fitted distribution) to answer questions about the measurement (calculating probabilities and percentiles etc).



Comparing Models


The results (answers) we find, depend on the assumptions we make!

The results we find when we evaluating a deterministic function at a specific input value will depend on the model we assume (and fit). These results will not be the same for all functions we choose to fit.
The results we find when we solve a deterministic function equal to a specific output value will depend on the model we assume (and fit). These results will not be the same for all functions we choose to fit.
The results we find when we calculate a probability will depend on the model we assume (and fit). The results from a Uniform random variable will not the same as the results from a Normal, Gamma, or Exponential distribution.
The results we find when we calculate a percentile will depend on the model we assume (and fit). The results from a Uniform random variable will not the same as the results from a Normal, Gamma, or Exponential distribution.

Just because we assume a certain general model, does not actually mean that model is a good way to model the measurement or relationship we want to study.

So you might ask, - How do we decide what models to assume? (or How do we decide which model to use?) - How do we compare multiple fitted models? - How do we use information from multiple fitted models? - How do we use information from fitting the same models to multiple data sets measuring the same process? I am pleased you recognize there is much to learn about using models in data analysis. You will need to take additional classes and keep gathering information and tools to answer that question. Keep learning. Mathematics can help us answer many questions but you have to put in the time if you want to use the tools. One could make an argument that learning to read Mathematics is more important than learning a particular type of mathematics because if you can read you can learn. Don’t be intimidated by the symbols remember that is where the information is in mathematics, take it slow and work through the meaning of the symbols.
